region_of_origin: Malaysia / Singapore (Zi Char tradition with Thai culinary influence)
food_subgroup: Cephalopod seafood dishes — steamed squid with spicy-sour sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class but no specific entry for steamed whole squid with Thai-style spicy-sour sauce. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers squids and cuttlefishes as a category. No specific code exists for the prepared Thai-style steamed dish; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for steamed squid dish. none assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving consists of one whole squid (approximately 300 g including sauce) presented on a plate, suitable for sharing between two diners as part of a Zi Char multi-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base squid nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy steamed/moist-heat squid (NDB 15150). Sauce components (fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, garlic, sugar) contribute additional sodium (~150 mg/100g estimated) and minor carbohydrate (~2 g/100g estimated). Final per-100g values represent the composite dish including sauce, estimated by proportional ingredient weighting. Confidence is medium due to sauce contribution estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed squid with Thai-style spicy-sour sauce. GI estimated at 30 based on analogy with cooked seafood and mixed seafood dishes (GI ~30–40 range per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). The dish is predominantly protein with low carbohydrate content; the sauce contributes modest sugar and lime juice. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 13.5) / 100 = 4.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1440 mg per 300 g serving) represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed squid with Thai-style spicy-sour sauce has a low estimated GI of approximately 30 and a low glycemic load of approximately 4 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake from fish sauce is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol content in squid offset by low saturated fat; elevated sodium from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 233 mg per 100 g) but low in saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware of the high sodium content from fish sauce and moderate portion sizes accordingly, while benefiting from the lean protein and omega-3 polyunsaturated fat profile of squid.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Whole Squid in Thai-Style Spicy and Sour Sauce is a seafood dish popular in Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char (煮炒) restaurants, where whole squid is gently steamed to preserve its tender texture and then dressed with a vibrant Thai-inspired sauce made from fish sauce, fresh lime juice, minced garlic, sliced fresh chilli, coriander, and a touch of sugar. The dish is characterised by a bold interplay of spicy, sour, salty, and lightly sweet flavours, with the squid remaining succulent and slightly chewy. The sauce is poured over the whole squid just before serving, allowing the aromatics to perfume the flesh. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (approximately 17.9 g per 100 g), low in fat (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g), and moderate in carbohydrate (approximately 4.5 g per 100 g), primarily from the sauce. It is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. The sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 30), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal. It is commonly served as a shared dish in a multi-course Zi Char meal alongside rice and vegetables.
common_user_queries: How many calories in steamed squid with Thai sauce?
Is Thai style steamed squid good for diabetics?
What is Zi Char steamed squid?
Is steamed squid high in sodium?
Can I eat steamed squid if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of steamed squid?

confidence_reason: Base squid nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy (steamed squid, moist heat), which is an approved primary source. Sauce component contributions (sodium, carbohydrate, sugar) are estimated by proportional ingredient weighting and are not directly measured. GI is an analogous estimate from approved peer-reviewed sources. No exact match for this specific dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry and is not reliably estimated for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for moist-heat cooked squid). Sodium value of 480 mg/100g is an adapted estimate combining USDA squid base sodium (~260 mg/100g for moist-heat squid) with estimated sauce contribution (~220 mg/100g from fish sauce, lime, and seasoning). GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. The dish is served at Marina Seafood House - Boat Quay (field survey data) at SGD 32.00. The cuisine is classified as Malaysian Zi Char with Thai culinary influence. Multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai, consistent with the Singaporean/Malaysian context of the dish and the Thai culinary style referenced in the dish name.
